speaker: interviewer
text: Describing a selection-- would you tell me more about that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in this-- it was called [POLISH]. It was really-- it came-- [POLISH] is the Polish word for it. It means cauldron.

speaker: interviewer
text: It means what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cauldron-- C-A-U-L-D-R-O-N. And it comes from a German word. This was called [GERMAN], you know? And [GERMAN] was being surrounded, being, being taken into a small place. But somehow from that, it came into Polish that the castle is like a cauldron. And that's what they called this one experience. It was in September of 1942, and where all people had to go to, to this particular place where the selection was taking place. And only people who worked at the time had some ID card, some statement, some certificate that they were working, supposedly were left in the ghetto. And the other ones were deported. Now there were some people who never went when the call came to come, to come to this place, who didn't come and were hiding. But many of them were killed. Because the Germans and the police and there was Latvian or Ukrainian police going from building to building and searching for people who were, were in the apartments. So some people hid and didn't go. But most of the people had to go. And there were some, there were some horrid things were going on at this, at this time, at this so-called selection. I remember children were not illegal anymore and-- cause they were not productive. So we had-- so some people were trying to smuggle their children through the-- in, in knapsacks. They gave them something to sleep and-- because there was still pharmacists in, in the ghetto. And they knew how to, you know-- they had some pills and whatnot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the, I, I saw myself there was some man who carried a child with-- in his knapsack. And somehow, there were some Germans. And then noticed that. And the child moved. And they made the man take the knapsack off. And then they, they shot the, the child. And they shot the man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand that later on, they had these Latvian SS men go. And when people had some knapsack, they would, with the bayonet, go and that to just see if there were any, you know, children. And kill it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, that's what people told who got the through with it. And my mother was in a different column. She also said-- she said that she had to step over some dead people, because they were shooting right in the, in this column. And she had to step over. And so anyway, we, we did make it, my father and I. And we went back to [? Schultz. ?] And later on, after many, many hours, my mother came. She made it through also. And-- but at that time, there were about 120,000 Jews left in the ghetto. Because all the others from almost half a million, many, many thousands had died of typhus and starvation. And then as they were deporting 10,000 a day, so it went down to about, by September, about 120,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any idea at that time what deportation meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We, we kind of knew it was something very bad, you know, the going east. In the beginning, we-- the Germans were saying if you volunteer, you can stay together with your family. And hunger was so great. That rations were cut down to about 184 calories. And so hunger was so great that when they said you can get two kilos of bread and a kilo of marmalade, people would just go for that and almost volunteer. But later, the rumors came that, you know, you going to go. They were talking about the camp and that people were being killed in the camp. And people didn't want to believe it. They couldn't believe it. And they even thought that these people who are saying these things, they are provocateurs. They want, want an uprising and want to give the Germans an excuse to come in and kill people. But later on, slowly, we realized that something horrid is going on. We didn't know about gas chambers. But we did know about people being shot in mass and put in mass graves, like they had to dig their own. So that, that knowledge came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. Some people were talking about it, that they were-- because, you know, in the beginning, the people who were deported in the beginning were forced to write postcards that they are working everything. They are working hard. But it's better than being in the ghetto, and that they had to send these postcards just before they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean these-- they were sent by the Germans. And all you needed is three, four postcards and the news spread like wildfire, you know? Oh, so and so heard that. So and so got a card that it's, it's OK. You know, it was just constant. We had constant rumors and so all we needed is a few of these postcards. And you went-- you believed until the other rumors started to come in that it's not true. So anyway, when this [POLISH,] when that cauldron started, there were about 100-- approximately 120,000 Jews. And about 60,000 were taken in these few-- in two days. Well, for us, it was two days. Because it was really going on for, I think, four or five days of that selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we worked in this-- at [? Schultz. ?] And I-- my mother was telling me that where she worked-- she worked with uniforms-- so they, they were trying to hide some children. And they put some children in waste baskets. And the kids had to stay in there for the whole eight, nine hours. There was a curfew during the day. Nobody was allowed to be in the street. Because everybody was working. And then we got a, an hour or two that we could move around. And then it was curfew at night again. Well, at one time, I remember the other selection that came to [? Schultz. ?] And they had everybody line up in the street. And it was a hot day. And I was-- and the German SS man was done back and forth and just pointing to different people and selecting them out. And I was standing next to my father. And my father was very, you know, tired. And he was slumping. And this German, this SS man, kept us there for hours. And so I was afraid that he might take my father. So I would poke in the back whenever the German was approaching. And my father had to straighten out. I would poke him in the ribs, in the back. And whenever the-- and I would rub my cheeks. And I would tell my father to rub his cheeks when the German wasn't looking, so as to look healthier. A few people tried to escape. They started to run. And they were shot immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had decided that, you know, if he would point to me or something, I would start running and so, so to be, to be shot. But at that time, you know, it was really very, very bad. And we were in constant fear of deportation. And we were hungry. And it, it was just horrid. And so then, then my folks decided to, to escape. Oh, you know, in the ghetto, too-- I forgot to tell you-- and we had to give in, give our fur coats, you know? And the people would cut thick fur up. But, but they still had to bring it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were people who were denouncing others for some things-- you know, they were hoping to get some more food for it or something. So they-- so if somebody knew that you had a fur coat, you felt obligated to give that fur coat in. Because you were afraid that somebody might denounce you that you didn't give it. But you would rip the, the seams and do things to the-- cut it. And so we-- that's why I had mentioned my fur coat, my mother's fur coat, my fur coat, when. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any heat, any coal or wood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I remember being very cold. And-- but I don't remember how this-- how the apartments were heated also. I do know that people were taking wood from the staircases, the, the railings, and chopping that up for wood in the winter. And there was no, no light. I remember that small [NON-ENGLISH] light, lamps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I somehow, I don't remember that-- all I remember being is cold, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and hungry. And then when we escaped, I escaped through-- on a truck. My father had bribed a, a, a driver, a German driver. And he slowed down at certain spot. And I jumped out. And I went to a friend, a Gentile friend, and hid there. And this friend gave me-- I was able to, to, to get a false ID card. And the ID card was on the name of [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE] was the name of an aunt of mine who had made a Gentile Pole. And the idea was that you take a name that you won't forget so easily. You know, if you-- all of a sudden, you get a very strange name and if you're in hot water, you might forget what the name was. So we took the name of [INAUDIBLE]. My mother escaped with some smugglers. And she had some horrendous experience, too. She-- at one point, she got separated from these smugglers who knew where the, where the hole was through which they were going through. And there were some policemen, Polish policemen, that was near that hole that was being bribed. And the Jewish policeman was bribed. And anyway, my mother didn't know where that, that hole was to get--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this through the sewer system?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- no, not this. That was under the-- through a hole in the wall. You know, there was some kind of a small hole. Matter of fact, my mother wrote some memoirs just before she died. And she describes her escape. And it's just horrendous. She-- anyway, she had to go through a part of a no-man-- so-called no-man's land. It was all bombed out. It was still part of the ghetto. But nobody lived there. And she-- it was pretty dark. But she was trying to find the, these smugglers and the, the hole. And all of sudden, the Germans-- she heard the German motorcycle. So she, she ran. And she ran into some building that was bombed out. And she fell into some kind of a hole. And there was, was a puddle. It was in November. So anyway, she, she fell into that hole. And the two Germans came with the flashlight. And they were going, you know, back and forth with the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And one was saying to the other, I, I saw her. I saw somebody run. I saw. And my mother was just kind of squeezed at the, at the wall, you know? And they were just about above her. But they didn't look straight down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said she was praying. She said, if there, if there is a God, please help me. Please help me. Because, you know, nobody will ever know where I died but tell my child and my husband. Anyway, so apparently God helped her. And they didn't, didn't find, didn't discover her. And then she made her way to, again, some Gentile friends. No actually it wasn't Gentile. They were-- the, the woman was Jewish. But she was married to a non-Jew. And she never went into the ghetto. And her husband had-- was an officer in the Polish army. And he was in a POW camp. But she had some kind of a lover, another Polish man. And he was helping out with the ID cards, with the very false ID cards. Anyway, so I was out. My mother was out. She was able to get-- rented peasant's hut, half a hut. And there were just rooms, a kitchen and living and, and another room. And we build or made a hole under the, under the floor. We dug out the, the ground. And then had to put it in the, in the bushes and forest at night, the, the dirt that we dug out. And that was the hole for my father to hide when he came out. In the meantime, I got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a job at the railroad. I saw in the newspaper in an ad that they're looking for somebody with knowledge of German and, you know, Polish. So I went. And I applied for the job. And it was, was kind of strange. I went to this railroad administration building, the personnel director. And he set me down. And he said, OK. I'm going to dictate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But-- no, I'm, I'm skipping something. But anyway, he, he dictated something. And I was typing with my, you know, two fingers. And I-- and it was a, a very technical thing about the-- I'll never forget it-- about the brake system. And one word was as long as a whole line and one sentence as long as a paragraph.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I swear, there must have been some angel sitting on my, on my shoulder. Because I did well. And he took it and read it. And then he says to me, you say you're Polish? And I said, yes. And he said, impossible. And I said, no, I'm, I'm Polish, you know? And, and he kept on and on, like, you, you're not. And I said, I am. So I-- then I thought-- then I got scared. And I thought that he probably suspects me of being Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Jews usually-- many, at least many, Jews were better educated, you know, and knew languages. So I thought, uh-oh. He suspects me. And so I, so I, I had this story made up. I said finally, well, yes I'm Polish. But my mother was a German, she was. And-- but she died when I, when I was born. And I grew up with my German grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the question comes up, why aren't you a Volksdeutsch? Volksdeutsch is a ethnic German. So I had made up a story about my father being a Polish officer. And he was in a POW camp. So I couldn't be a Volksdeutsch. And anyway, he was so thrilled with himself that I-- that he recognized I wasn't a pure-blooded Pole, that I had German blood in me, that he, he said, where do you want to work? And I said, what do you mean, where? Where do you have the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, no. Just tell me where you want to work. And at that time, my parents were hiding in Otwock. So I said, well, Otwock then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gets on the phone. And he calls this station master. And he says, I'm sending a young woman over. And you give her a job tomorrow. She's starting tomorrow. So with that, you know, I went to my job the next day. And the station master, who was a German. I don't know, must have been in his 50s. He greeted me. He was very polite. He gave me a desk and like a cubicle, like almost like an office to myself, and, and nothing to do. And I sat there for three days. He gave me some magazines. And I thought, gee, that's strange, you know? I mean, what-- why, why am I here? But I didn't say anything. I was a little scared. And so then he-- one day, he comes. And he says to me, Heinz or Hans-- I forgot-- is coming. And I said who is Heinz? And he laughs, you know, this knowing smile. And he says, you mean you don't know who Heinz is? I said, no. Who is Heinz? And he looks again. And he winks and whatnot, you know, like a knowing wink. And I said, I don't know. So he said, you really don't know? I said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's the personnel director. And he thought that I must have been the personnel director's mistress. And so, so that's why he treated me like this. And then when I told him I really-- I got the job legitimately, so he took me out from this little cubicle, gave me a desk and a pile of work like that I had to do. And so I worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you alter your appearance in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, my-- once I got out of the ghetto, I bleached my hair. And I was a, I was a blonde. Matter of fact, when I got out, soon afterwards, a very good friend of mine got out. And she came to me. And I bleached her hair. And-- but she was so scared. She couldn't take life outside of the ghetto. After a few days, she went back. And I understand-- somebody told me that she died a heroic death, throwing Molotov cocktails during the uprising. Because you know, you were outside. And you were in constant, constant fear. Because there were always these hoodlums, these blackmailers, and who either-- well, always on the lookout for a Jew, mainly to shake you down for money. But frequently also, after they shook you down, they would take you to the police or to the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So especially for a girl-- you know, when I walked in the street and I was a young girl. How old was I-- 17, 18. So some men looked at me and looked at my legs or something. And I didn't know, does he suspect me or not? I am afraid to turn around to see if he still stands there and looks at, looks at me. And I had a good figure, not like now, but you know? So I was kind of attractive. So sometimes very innocently, somebody might look at you. But your heart is pumping. And your adrenaline is, is going. So you were in constant fear. And she couldn't take that fear. She felt more secure in the ghetto. So she went back. Where were you living then, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in, in Otwock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I subletted a, a room. And this room was just connected with a-- it was a big apartment. And this one room was sublet. It had separate entrance and also a door to the apartment, which was closed. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to see your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Well, I was in constant touch with, with them. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they both in the, the hole underneath the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. While they were on that whole time, my mother would go out, because she was a woman. And a woman was, was much safer. Because for a man, if you catch a man, all you had to do is tell him to remove his pants. Because they were circumcised. And the Gentile Poles were not circumcised. It's not like here, where many Gentiles are circumcised. So that was proof positive. With a woman, you had to really kind of prove-- take her in to Gestapo and look at the papers and, and whatnot, and, and sometime torture. But you know, but it was easier for a, a woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother was walking freely and just to-- she had to be busy with something or show that she's earning a living. And she was very good at knitting. So she made it known in this, in, in this town there that she accepts old sweaters and stuff. And she would rip them up and knit and make scarves and gloves and, and socks, and you know, pretend that that's her only source of income.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father never left their house. And just in times of danger, he would hide. When the peasant who rented my-- this place would come in, my father-- there was one big closet also. He would either go into this closet when there was not enough time or, or go into that hole. And-- well, there is-- I have a few of kind of funny stories to tell. When I first came out, I had my ID card on [INAUDIBLE]. But it wasn't good. Because there was nothing to back it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the friend that supplied that card?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his name was Czesiak. And he was active also in the underground. I had known him from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know to go to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we stayed in touch. He even came-- in the beginning, he even came into the ghetto to visit me. And he, he-- matter of fact, I found out recently that he became a very known science fiction writer and was very well-known in, in Poland after the war. But anyway, he was my first contact. And-- but as I say, that ID card was no good. And then through somebody else, we got a, a birth certificate and baptismal certificate from a town that was destroyed, was on the Russian side, in the Ukraine somewhere. And this was under the name of [INAUDIBLE]. And with birth certificate and, and baptismal certificate, I went to the administration. I don't remember what it was called. But I submitted it. And on the basis of that, I got another ID card on that name of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we continue this story on the next tape, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Gee, I--
